quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-sample t-test"?

options.text: One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)
With replacement: draws are independent; without replacement: draws are dependent because the population changes after each draw
The average of the squared deviations from the mean, calculated using n-1 in the denominator for sample data (Bessel's correction)
Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-sample t-test" is defined as: Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-sample t-interval"?

options.text: After forming blocks, using a chance mechanism to assign treatments within each block to balance remaining confounders
x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); a confidence interval for the population mean using the t-distribution critical value
The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes
The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-sample t-interval" is defined as: x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); a confidence interval for the population mean using the t-distribution critical value. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conditions for t-procedures"?

options.text: Two events A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), meaning knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A
Random sample, 10% condition (n < 10% of population), and Normal/Large Sample (population approximately normal or n ≥ 30)
The distribution of one variable given a specific value of another; found by dividing cell counts by the appropriate row or column total
Systematic error caused by the wording of questions, interviewer influence, or respondent dishonesty leading to inaccurate answers

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conditions for t-procedures" is defined as: Random sample, 10% condition (n < 10% of population), and Normal/Large Sample (population approximately normal or n ≥ 30). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "t* critical value"?

options.text: A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable
A branching diagram showing sequential probabilities along paths; multiplying along a path gives joint probability, adding across paths gives marginal probability
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1
The t-value with area C in the middle of the t-distribution with df = n-1; found from t-table or technology for the desired confidence level

quiz_questions.explanation: "t* critical value" is defined as: The t-value with area C in the middle of the t-distribution with df = n-1; found from t-table or technology for the desired confidence level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of t-Distribution and t-Procedures, which best defines "Standard error of x̄"?

options.text: t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions
A branching diagram showing sequential probabilities along paths; multiplying along a path gives joint probability, adding across paths gives marginal probability
SE = s/√n; estimates the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄ using the sample standard deviation s
A variable that takes a finite or countably infinite number of specific values, each with a definite probability

quiz_questions.explanation: In t-Distribution and t-Procedures, "Standard error of x̄" refers to: SE = s/√n; estimates the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄ using the sample standard deviation s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown"?

options.text: Marginal distribution
Required sample size
Sample proportion (p̂)
One-sample t-test

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-sample t-test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); a confidence interval for the population mean using the t-distribution critical value"?

options.text: Stratified random sample
One-sample t-interval
Margin of error
Homogeneity vs. independence

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-sample t-interval".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Random sample, 10% condition (n < 10% of population), and Normal/Large Sample (population approximately normal or n ≥ 30)"?

options.text: LINE conditions
Multiplication rule for independents
Confidence interval for p
Conditions for t-procedures

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conditions for t-procedures".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The t-value with area C in the middle of the t-distribution with df = n-1; found from t-table or technology for the desired confidence level"?

options.text: Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Two-way table
Type II error
t* critical value

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "t* critical value".

quiz_questions.question_text: In t-Distribution and t-Procedures, which term matches: "SE = s/√n; estimates the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄ using the sample standard deviation s"?

options.text: Z-score
Response bias
Standard error of x̄
Stratified random sample

quiz_questions.explanation: In t-Distribution and t-Procedures, this refers to "Standard error of x̄".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about t-Distribution and t-Procedures is accurate?

options.text: Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
Significance Tests for Slopes test H₀: β₁ = 0 (no linear relationship) using t = b₁/SE(b₁) with df = n-2.
t-Distribution and t-Procedures provide the inferential framework for means when the population standard deviation is unknown.
Sampling Methods are systematic procedures for selecting a subset of individuals from a population to draw conclusions about the whole.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distribution and t-Procedures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: Tree diagrams and two-way tables provide systematic visual methods for computing conditional probabilities in complex scenarios.

Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.

The one-sample t-statistic t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) follows a t-distribution with df = n-1 under H₀. t-procedures include one-sample t-tests (testing a claim about μ) and one-sample t-intervals (estimating μ), both requiring random sampling and...
Understanding the difference between theoretical, empirical, and subjective probability is essential for statistical reasoning.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distribution and t-Procedures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: If the population has mean μ and standard deviation σ, then the sampling distribution of x̄ has mean μx̄ = μ and standard deviation σx̄ = σ/√n (the standard error).

Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
t-Distribution and t-Procedures provide the inferential framework for means when the population standard deviation is unknown.
A well-designed experiment with random assignment allows researchers to attribute differences in response to the treatment rather than confounding variables.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distribution and t-Procedures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: The calculation is identical to the independence test: χ² = Σ(O-E)²/E with df = (r-1)(c-1) and E = (row total × column total)/grand total.
Data collection methods—including census, survey, and experiment—each have distinct strengths and limitations that affect the validity and generalizability of conclusions.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.

The one-sample t-statistic t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) follows a t-distribution with df = n-1 under H₀. t-procedures include one-sample t-tests (testing a claim about μ) and one-sample t-intervals (estimating μ), both requiring random sampling and...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distribution and t-Procedures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: Common transformations include log(y) vs. x for exponential relationships and log(y) vs. log(x) for power relationships, with back-transformation needed to interpret predictions in original units.
Conditional probability is fundamental to updating beliefs with new information and is the basis of Bayes' Theorem, which reverses conditional probabilities.
Sampling Methods are systematic procedures for selecting a subset of individuals from a population to draw conclusions about the whole.
t-Distribution and t-Procedures provide the inferential framework for means when the population standard deviation is unknown.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distribution and t-Procedures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: Comparing median household income (resistant to billionaire outliers) versus mean income when evaluating economic policy and wealth distribution
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories
A nutritionist testing whether the mean sodium content of a product exceeds 500 mg using a sample of 15 packages and a one-sample t-test
Hospital data examining whether survival rates (survived/died) are conditionally distributed differently across different treatment regimens

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: Engineering firms constructing a 95% t-interval for the mean breaking strength of a new alloy based on testing 20 specimens
Airport security using sampling distributions to determine how many bags to inspect (sample size) to achieve a desired precision in estimating the rate of prohibited items
A manufacturer finding a significant chi-square result for defect type vs. production shift, then identifying that the night shift drives most of the association
Climate scientists testing whether year is a significant predictor of global temperature anomaly (testing for a statistically significant warming trend)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is t-Distribution and t-Procedures applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: A/B testing in technology companies randomly assigning users to different website designs to determine which produces higher engagement
Educational researchers using a one-sample t-test to determine whether average ACT scores in a district differ from the national mean of 21
Modeling population growth of bacteria over time using exponential transformation, then predicting colony size at future time points
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of t-Distribution and t-Procedures?

options.text: Market research testing whether customer color preferences match the company's assumed distribution to guide product design decisions
Climate scientists testing whether year is a significant predictor of global temperature anomaly (testing for a statistically significant warming trend)
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution
A nutritionist testing whether the mean sodium content of a product exceeds 500 mg using a sample of 15 packages and a one-sample t-test

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows t-Distribution and t-Procedures in practice?

options.text: Pharmaceutical trials determining sample size needed so the sampling distribution of the cure rate is narrow enough to detect a clinically meaningful effect
Engineering firms constructing a 95% t-interval for the mean breaking strength of a new alloy based on testing 20 specimens
Analyzing the power relationship between body mass and metabolic rate in animals using log-log transformation
Epidemiological prospective cohort studies (like the Framingham Heart Study) tracking thousands of people over decades to identify cardiovascular risk factors

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distribution and t-Procedures.
